Drylands & GenderA DSL-IP publication · FAO & GEF

Field notes · Malawi

When women lead, the land recovers

In Malawi's drylands, the DSL-IP is proving that restoring degraded landscapes and closing gender gaps are not two goals; they are one. Here is what gender-responsive restoration looks like on the ground.

GC
DSL-IP Gender Community of Practice · CoP3
9 min read · Updated June 2026

For most of her adult life, Belabila Makoni planted trees without quite knowing why. A 43-year-old widow raising two children in Bonongwe Village, Traditional Authority Chilipa, she joined the Masakasa Forest and Farmer Producer Organization in 2015 to combat the degradation creeping across her community's land, establishing nurseries, planting, and nurturing natural regeneration. The benefit to her own household, she admits, was not obvious at first.

"When I first joined, I did not fully understand how I would benefit from the group. Even when we were advised to establish family woodlots to lead by example, I did so without fully understanding the benefits." Belabila Makoni · Masakasa FFPO · Mangochi District

That changed in 2023. When the Drylands Sustainable Landscapes Impact Program (DSL-IP) reached her landscape, Makoni was trained in climate-smart agriculture, intercropping, and establishing home woodlots. The techniques reshaped her farming, and her prospects with it.

"Since implementing what I learned, my life has never been the same. In 2024, I harvested twice as much as in previous seasons, which has greatly improved my household food security and income." Belabila Makoni

Her resilience held even as the rains arrived late and dry spells stretched on. Makoni's story is not an exception the program stumbled upon; it is the design working as intended. In the DSL-IP's Malawi project, gender is not a box ticked at the end of a logframe. It is built into how restoration is planned, taught, financed, and measured.

One plot, two harvests, and a lighter load for women

The Malawi project sits within a deceptively technical name: sustainable land and forest management toward land degradation neutrality through integrated food and energy systems, or IFES for short. In practice, IFES is elegant. Maize is intercropped with pigeon pea so that a single plot yields food and the woody biomass a household burns for fuel. Restore the soil, feed the family, and grow the firewood in the same field.

That last point carries quiet gender weight. In dryland Malawi, gathering fuelwood is overwhelmingly women's work, and it grows harder as forests thin. By moving the fuel source onto the farm, IFES gives back the hours women would otherwise spend walking for wood, hours that can go to enterprise, to learning, or to rest. It is a reminder that the most powerful gender interventions are often embedded in the agronomy itself.

Restoration that runs on producer organizations

The engine of the work is the Forest and Farm Producer Organization (FFPO): community-led groups, like Makoni's, that turn conservation into livelihood. Under the Sustainable Landscape Production (SLP) framework, the component of DSL-IP where gender results are deliberately generated, the project has built these organizations into vehicles for women's economic empowerment.

2,740
members across 36 prioritized producer organizations, including 906 women and over 1,400 youth
2,398
members now active in green value-chain enterprises, 701 of them women
320
members trained in beekeeping & fish farming: 189 women and 131 men

Every value-chain target carries a gender floor: at least one third of the 2,000 members adopting improved post-harvest technologies, and of the 10,000 who will ultimately commercialize target commodities, must be women. These are not aspirations buried in a report; they are tracked, sex-disaggregated, in the project's annual implementation review.

Green value chains, designed around women's realities

This year the project published a Green Value Chain training manual for the extension workers who support producer organizations. It is unusually candid about gender. Processing methods are chosen to ease the burden on women, with alternative techniques for shelling pigeon peas, for instance, while explicitly recognizing women as the primary processors. Market analyses fold in gender considerations, and business-plan training is delivered with gender parity as a working principle, not an afterthought.

Seed banks where women's knowledge counts

Restoration depends on what gets planted, and that knowledge is gendered. The project's Integrated Capacity Development and Implementation Plan, assembled from Integrated Landscape Assessment Methodology (ILAM) results and tailored household surveys, devotes an entire curriculum module to crop diversity and gender. Using a "diversity wheel," facilitators map how women and men value different varieties differently, so that community seed banks conserve the crops people actually want to grow.

Field schools, too, are tuned to women's labour constraints: intercropping and soil-and-water techniques are selected to be less labour-intensive and genuinely usable by women, and women's community networks are treated as channels for diffusion rather than passive recipients of training.

The most durable gender gains are not separate "women's activities" bolted onto a restoration project; they are restoration practices designed, from the first module, around how women actually live and work.

Changing the rules, not just the fields

Practice without policy rarely lasts. So the project is also reshaping the rules that govern land. Working through the Centre for Environmental Policy and Advocacy, it has begun training district councils to draft gender-inclusive by-laws and regulations for land use and tenure, with a target that half of the village leaders trained in policy formulation are women, and that gender-inclusive rules reach the large majority of target communities. Training is underway in the first of three districts.

Why it matters

From uncertain volunteer to resilient producer

Belabila Makoni · Masakasa FFPO · Bonongwe Village, T/A Chilipa, Mangochi

Belabila Makoni joined her producer organization in 2015 and added beekeeping in 2018, but honey sales alone never covered her family's needs. Training in climate-smart agriculture, intercropping and home woodlots in 2023 doubled her 2024 harvest and steadied her income through a difficult, dry season.

"Since implementing what I learned, my life has never been the same."

Her arc, from uncertain volunteer to confident producer, is the human shape of a sex-disaggregated indicator. It is what "at least one third women" looks like when it reaches a single household.

What this adds up to

Read against the DSL-IP's global gender gaps (unequal access to natural resources, unbalanced participation in decisions, and uneven access to economic benefits), Malawi's project pushes on all three at once. IFES returns resources and time to women; gender-inclusive by-laws open the door to decision-making; and green value chains widen access to income. None of it is loud. It is the patient, structural work of making restoration and equity the same project.

There is a long way to go. Implementation has faced delays, and most targets stretch out to the program's final years. But the direction is set, and it is visible in a doubled harvest in Bonongwe Village. When women lead the way, the land recovers with them.
